tossups.question: In 1960, Ida Gordon became the first scholar to author a standalone translation of this poem. J. R. R. Tolkien’s unfinished story “The Lost Road” contains the poem “Monað (“MO-nath”) Modes Lust mid Mereflode,” which is based on this poem. G.V. Smithers argued that the word “anfloga” in this poem refers to a Valkyrie. The ambiguous word “Sylf” appears in this poem’s first line, which, along with Beowulf, is the only known text to contain the word “unwearnum.” A loose translation of this poem that only goes up to 99 lines states “Bosque taketh blossom, cometh beauty of berries,” and “Cuckoo calleth with gloomy crying,” whose alliteration Allen Ginsberg contrasted with the translation’s last line of “Be an unlikely treasure hoard.” The last line of this poem, whose speaker is likely in exile, contains only the word (*) “Amen.” This poem’s first line was rendered as “May I for my own self song’s truth reckon” in a translation by Ezra pound. For 10 points, name this Exeter Book poem where the speaker narrates about being alone on the water.
tossups.question_sanitized: In 1960, Ida Gordon became the first scholar to author a standalone translation of this poem. J. R. R. Tolkien's unfinished story "The Lost Road" contains the poem "Monað ("MO-nath") Modes Lust mid Mereflode," which is based on this poem. G.V. Smithers argued that the word "anfloga" in this poem refers to a Valkyrie. The ambiguous word "Sylf" appears in this poem's first line, which, along with Beowulf, is the only known text to contain the word "unwearnum." A loose translation of this poem that only goes up to 99 lines states "Bosque taketh blossom, cometh beauty of berries," and "Cuckoo calleth with gloomy crying," whose alliteration Allen Ginsberg contrasted with the translation's last line of "Be an unlikely treasure hoard." The last line of this poem, whose speaker is likely in exile, contains only the word (*) "Amen." This poem's first line was rendered as "May I for my own self song's truth reckon" in a translation by Ezra pound. For 10 points, name this Exeter Book poem where the speaker narrates about being alone on the water.

tossups.question: In a poem in this language, a "wise man" muses how "all permanence rests" in the heavens after recounting men who "the bird took off... the gray wolf [shared] with death." A poem in this language describes the churning of butter with innuendo like "push[ing] something stiff [as] the two of them shook." A "shaggy below" object that helps women is revealed to be an onion in a riddle poem in this language. The line "may I for my own self song's truth reckon" opens an inaccurate Ezra Pound translation which tries to preserve this language's alliterative verse. The figurative compound word "earth-stepper" is used to refer to a poem's title "Wanderer" in one of this language's kennings. For 10 points, name this language used for the poems of the Exeter Book<u> and Beowulf</u>.
tossups.question_sanitized: In a poem in this language, a "wise man" muses how "all permanence rests" in the heavens after recounting men who "the bird took off... the gray wolf [shared] with death." A poem in this language describes the churning of butter with innuendo like "push[ing] something stiff [as] the two of them shook." A "shaggy below" object that helps women is revealed to be an onion in a riddle poem in this language. The line "may I for my own self song's truth reckon" opens an inaccurate Ezra Pound translation which tries to preserve this language's alliterative verse. The figurative compound word "earth-stepper" is used to refer to a poem's title "Wanderer" in one of this language's kennings. For 10 points, name this language used for the poems of the Exeter Book and Beowulf.
tossups.answer_sanitized: Old English [or Anglo-Saxon; do not accept "English"]

tossups.question: An author nicknamed for this sort of creature earned the epithet “the homilist” for a tract condemning his countrymen for incurring raids. The Wife’s Lament is often compared to a poem in which a woman longs for a man named for this creature over her husband Eadwacer (“ADD-wah-chair”). A raven, an eagle, and this creature are the three standard “beasts of battle.” A poet partially named for this creature penned the long poems “Christ II” and “Juliana,” which appear in the (*) Exeter Book; that poet’s name combines “Cyne” (“KIN-uh”) with this animal. Henry Sweet suggested that “bee” and this animal made up a word that was a kenning for “bear” and names a man who wields the sword Hrunting to avenge the mead-hall Heorot. For 10 points, the name of the Old English hero who slays Grendel combines “Beo” and what animal?
tossups.question_sanitized: An author nicknamed for this sort of creature earned the epithet "the homilist" for a tract condemning his countrymen for incurring raids. The Wife's Lament is often compared to a poem in which a woman longs for a man named for this creature over her husband Eadwacer ("ADD-wah-chair"). A raven, an eagle, and this creature are the three standard "beasts of battle." A poet partially named for this creature penned the long poems "Christ II" and "Juliana," which appear in the (*) Exeter Book; that poet's name combines "Cyne" ("KIN-uh") with this animal. Henry Sweet suggested that "bee" and this animal made up a word that was a kenning for "bear" and names a man who wields the sword Hrunting to avenge the mead-hall Heorot. For 10 points, the name of the Old English hero who slays Grendel combines "Beo" and what animal?
tossups.answer: wolf [or wulf; accept Wulfstan or Lupus; accept Cynewulf; accept Beowulf]
tossups.answer_sanitized: wolf [or wulf; accept Wulfstan or Lupus; accept Cynewulf; accept Beowulf]

tossups.question: The line “life is [one of these things], give it up” appears in the final couplet of a Hafez ode about being hungover. Arabic examples of these things include muʿammā (“moo-ah-mah”), whose invention is attributed to Abu Nuwas, as well as uhjiyya (“ooh-JEE-yeh”) and lughz (“loogz”). The writer Tatwine (“tah-TWEE-neh”) likely imitated the Epistola ad Acircium (“ay-PEE-stoh-lah ahd ah-KEER-kee-oom”) of Aldhelm, whose poetic examples of these things include De creatura (“day cray-ah-TOO-rah”) and the “Leiden” (“LAY-din”) one. Examples of these things concerning icebergs and onions are among the 94 of them in the (*) Exeter Book, written in Old English. The question “what’s in your pocket?” ends an exchange of these things in the dark between Bilbo and Gollum in The Hobbit. A line about a creature that stands on different numbers of legs is the sphinx’s example of, for 10 points, what quandaries?
tossups.answer: riddles [accept chistan; accept Leiden riddle; accept puzzles or quandaries or enigmas before “Exeter Book” and prompt on any of those answers after; prompt on questions]
tossups.answer_sanitized: riddles [accept chistan; accept Leiden riddle; accept puzzles or quandaries or enigmas before "Exeter Book" and prompt on any of those answers after; prompt on questions]

Examples of these things concerning icebergs and onions are among the 94 of them in the (*) Exeter Book, written in Old English. tossups.question: Two of these texts titled Lorica and Creatura appear in the Epistola of Aldhelm. Onions are claimed to be a “joy to women” in one of these texts. “Thirty white horses on a red hill” are described in one of these texts, over ninety of which appear in the Exeter Book. A character says, “suns on the daisies” in (*) response to one of these texts in a chapter of a novel titled for “[these texts] in the Dark.” The question “what have I got in my pocket?” is used as one of these texts by Gollum in a contest with Bilbo Baggins in The Hobbit. For 10 points, name these verbal puzzles that often have surprising answers.
tossups.question_sanitized: Two of these texts titled Lorica and Creatura appear in the Epistola of Aldhelm. Onions are claimed to be a "joy to women" in one of these texts. "Thirty white horses on a red hill" are described in one of these texts, over ninety of which appear in the Exeter Book. A character says, "suns on the daisies" in (*) response to one of these texts in a chapter of a novel titled for "[these texts] in the Dark." The question "what have I got in my pocket?" is used as one of these texts by Gollum in a contest with Bilbo Baggins in The Hobbit. For 10 points, name these verbal puzzles that often have surprising answers.
tossups.answer: riddles [accept “Riddles in the Dark”; prompt on questions or poems]

tossups.question: A poem titled for this character hails the morning star as Eärendil, the “brightest of angels.” This character’s “six leaps” are described in a poem titled for this character that, like the poems Juliana and Elene, contains runes that spell out the name “Cynewulf.” This character is the first of two in the title of a poem from a manuscript named for Franciscus Junius, who also discovered a 6,000-line Old Saxon poem about this character, The Heliand. The first (*) three poems of the Exeter Book are titled for this character. After burying this character, his followers cover the title object in gems, silver, and gold in the earliest surviving dream vision poem. For 10 points, name this character whose death is described in The Dream of the Rood.
tossups.question_sanitized: A poem titled for this character hails the morning star as Earendil, the "brightest of angels." This character's "six leaps" are described in a poem titled for this character that, like the poems Juliana and Elene, contains runes that spell out the name "Cynewulf." This character is the first of two in the title of a poem from a manuscript named for Franciscus Junius, who also discovered a 6,000-line Old Saxon poem about this character, The Heliand. The first (*) three poems of the Exeter Book are titled for this character. After burying this character, his followers cover the title object in gems, silver, and gold in the earliest surviving dream vision poem. For 10 points, name this character whose death is described in The Dream of the Rood.
tossups.answer: Jesus Christ [or Jesus Christ; prompt on equivalents of God]
tossups.answer_sanitized: Jesus Christ [or Jesus Christ; prompt on equivalents of God]

set.name: 2023 "Questions Based on Wikipedia Links Ophir Sent Me"

tossups.question: Negligence is called a “most stupid maiden” while Virginity is called a “most bright maiden” in a set of twenty of these works on the virtues and vices by Saint Boniface. One of these works describing an object produced from the “cold womb” of the “damp earth” is called either Lorica or the “Leiden” one of these works. One hundred of these works in Latin hexameter are included along with a treatise on the number seven in the Epistola ad Acircium, written by (+) Saint Aldhelm. One of these works concludes, “Her eye will be wet,” after describing an object which is “shaggy below” and “a wonderful help to women.” Several of these works, including ones about a butter churn, a key, and an onion, can also be interpreted as having (*) sexual meanings. For 10 points, name these works, over ninety of which are found in the Old English Exeter Book.
tossups.question_sanitized: Negligence is called a "most stupid maiden" while Virginity is called a "most bright maiden" in a set of twenty of these works on the virtues and vices by Saint Boniface. One of these works describing an object produced from the "cold womb" of the "damp earth" is called either Lorica or the "Leiden" one of these works. One hundred of these works in Latin hexameter are included along with a treatise on the number seven in the Epistola ad Acircium, written by (+) Saint Aldhelm. One of these works concludes, "Her eye will be wet," after describing an object which is "shaggy below" and "a wonderful help to women." Several of these works, including ones about a butter churn, a key, and an onion, can also be interpreted as having (*) sexual meanings. For 10 points, name these works, over ninety of which are found in the Old English Exeter Book.

tossups.question: A collection named for this type of person includes a poem whose speaker is a nun who “tends the gardens” and “prays for the immortal soul of rock ’n’ roll,” entitled “Elvis’s Twin Sister.” One of these people complains about being forced to live under an oak tree in an “earthen cave” in a “lament” found in the Exeter Book. In a play named after this type of person, Mrs. Squeamish and Sir Jasper Fidget are oblivious to the double entendres in Horner’s description of his china collection. After tearing several pages out of a book, one of these people is struck by Jankyn. That one of these people tells the story of a knight who, at the orders of Queen Guinevere, strikes a deal with an old woman to learn what it is that women desire most. For 10 points, one of the Canterbury Tales is told by what type of person from Bath?
tossups.answer: wife [or wives; accept The World’s Wife or “The Wife’s Lament” or The Country Wife or “The Wife of Bath’s Tale”]

packet.name: J (Texas A, Oxford B, UIUC C, and Maryland B)

tossups.question: Northrop Frye suggested that the aspect of poetry Aristotle called opsis is rooted in this sort of text, whereas melos is rooted in the charm. A medieval text of this sort uses phallic imagery to describe an onion being cut by a “proud woman”; that one is collected with over ninety of these texts in the Anglo-Saxon Exeter Book. One of these things that describes “Thirty white horses on a red hill” appears in a (*) chapter of The Hobbit titled for these things “in the dark,” in which Bilbo Baggins and Gollum exchange statements of this sort. In Alice and Wonderland, the Mad Hatter gives one of these things that consists of the question “Why is a raven like a writing-desk?” For 10 points, name this type of puzzling statement or question with an answer.
tossups.question_sanitized: Northrop Frye suggested that the aspect of poetry Aristotle called opsis is rooted in this sort of text, whereas melos is rooted in the charm. A medieval text of this sort uses phallic imagery to describe an onion being cut by a "proud woman"; that one is collected with over ninety of these texts in the Anglo-Saxon Exeter Book. One of these things that describes "Thirty white horses on a red hill" appears in a (*) chapter of The Hobbit titled for these things "in the dark," in which Bilbo Baggins and Gollum exchange statements of this sort. In Alice and Wonderland, the Mad Hatter gives one of these things that consists of the question "Why is a raven like a writing-desk?" For 10 points, name this type of puzzling statement or question with an answer.

tossups.question: An upstart scholar of this language at the University of Texas secures a speaking engagement at a conference by attacking the pedagogical methods of his senior colleague Ezra Winthrop in the story “The Bribe.” A biography by Edwin Williamson describes how an author and his students would triumphantly recite passages from this language in the streets that they spent Saturday mornings decoding at the Biblioteca Nacional. Martin Hadis and Martin Arias arranged for the transcription of lecture notes from a 1966 course at the University of Buenos Aires, whose first seven sections examine literature written in this language. (*) Borges never published his essay about Grímur Jónsson Thorkelin, who in 1787 had transcribed a poem in this language from its sole surviving copy in the Nowell Codex. The Book of Imaginary Beings discusses an island that turns out to be a whale, which its author had encountered in R. K. Gordon’s translation of this language’s bestiary in The Exeter Book. For 10 points, the tombstone of Borges is inscribed with a line from what language’s poem “The Battle of Maldon”?
tossups.answer: Old English [or Anglo-Saxon; prompt on English since Borges taught an English literature class]
tossups.answer_sanitized: Old English [or Anglo-Saxon; prompt on English since Borges taught an English literature class]

(*) Borges never published his essay about Grimur Jonsson Thorkelin, who in 1787 had transcribed a poem in this language from its sole surviving copy in the Nowell Codex. tossups.question: God summons one of these animals to devour corn after King Solomon prays to feed all the creatures in the world. The hunter Natsilane kills his brother-in-law with one of these animals after growing it from yellow cedar. A poem from the Exeter Book titled for one of these mammals describes an encounter with Fastitocalon, an Aspidochelone that behaves like another creature sometimes described as one of these mammals, the Jasconius encountered by Saint (*) Brendan. Big Raven eats mushrooms in order to gain enough strength to move one of these animals, which are preyed upon bythunderbirds. To escape Ruatapu, Paikea rides one of them in a Maori myth. Along with seals and walruses, they were born from the chopped fingers of Sedna. For 10 points, name these mammals exemplified by the Leviathan and the swallower of Jonah.
tossups.answer: whales [or cetaceans; antiprompt for order on specific members of Cetacea including dolphins; prompt for type of creature on sea monsters or similar answers]
tossups.answer_sanitized: whales [or cetaceans; antiprompt for order on specific members of Cetacea including dolphins; prompt for type of creature on sea monsters or similar answers]

tossups.question: The Exeter Book poem whose title mentions this tells of a king’s life before and after his fall from grace. One tract that argues for the usage of this technique was written in response to Thomas Campion’s Observations in the Art of English Poesie. That defense of it was written by Samuel Daniel. Shakespeare’s The Rape of Lucrece is written in a stanza format whose (*) name mentions this technique; that stanza consists of a tercet and two couplets and is called “[this] royale.” Depending on the number of syllables matched, this technique is classified as either “masculine” or “feminine.” For 10 points, name this technique of repeated similar sounds that is often found at the end of lines in poems.
tossups.answer: rhyme [Accept “The Rhyming Poem”, “A Defense of Rhyme,” “rhyme royal,” “masculine rhyme” and “feminine rhyme” at any point.]
tossups.answer_sanitized: rhyme [Accept "The Rhyming Poem", "A Defense of Rhyme," "rhyme royal," "masculine rhyme" and "feminine rhyme" at any point.]

bonuses.leadin: A poem titled for one of these people describes how all is quiet over the hills, before bidding the listener “Just wait: before long / You, too, shall rest.” For 10 points each:

parts: Name these people who title the aforementioned “Nightsong” by Goethe. An Old English poem from the Exeter Book titled for one of these people declares “Events always go as they must!”
One of the nightsongs addresses a person who “hails from” this place. The prologue to Goethe’s Faust is set in this place, whose army is commanded by Michael in Paradise Lost.

Goethe inscribed the lines of the second “Wanderer’s Nightsong” on the walls of one of these places. The speaker asks “whose name did it record / before mine?” after writing his name in a book at one of these places in a poem by Paul Celan.

bonuses.leadin: Craig Williamson claimed that the two “most perplexing” poems “in the entire Old English corpus” are Wulf and Eadwacer(“ADD-watcher”) and this poem. For 10 points each:
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: Craig Williamson claimed that the two "most perplexing" poems "in the entire Old English corpus" are Wulf and Eadwacer("ADD-watcher") and this poem. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this elegy from the Exeter Book whose speaker sings “each morning’s sorrow / Outside my earth-cave, under my oak tree” after her lover “left hearth and home, / Crossing the sea-road.”

bonuses.leadin: A five-line poem attributed to this author states “none is wiser than the man who considers [...] what judgement his soul will receive after its passing”. For 10 points each:

parts: Name this author who, according to Cuthbert, reportedly asked for “some pepper, and napkins, and some incense” before composing a poem on their deathbed on Ascension Day, 735.
Bede’s death song is the poem in this language that survives in the largest number of manuscripts. The Exeter Book contains many poems in this language used to write Beowulf.

The Exeter Book contains this 143-line poem which surveys the Heroic Age of Northern Europe. This poem gives a list of tribes and writes of how “Hrothwulf and Hrothgar held longest the kinship together”.

bonuses.leadin: Piers Plowman is typically grouped with a loose 14th-century “revival” named for this literary device, which included an account of the death of King Arthur usually titled for this device. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this literary device that was used to join two half-lines divided by a caesura (“suh-ZHUR-uh”) in most Old English poetry.
Alliteration was supplanted by this other technique, which names a prototypical 87-line poem found in the Exeter Book. This technique may be assigned “schemes” such as ABABA.

This poet, who is often grouped with the Alliterative Revival, used a device called a “bob and wheel” consisting of a pair of stressed syllables and a rhyming four-line stanza. In a poem attributed to this unknown author, a grieving father sees a maiden named for the title object.
answers: alliteration [or word forms such as alliterative; accept Alliterative Revival; accept Alliterative Morte Arthure]
rhyme [or word forms such as rhyming; accept “The Rhyming Poem” or “The Riming Poem”; accept end rhyme or other forms of rhyme; accept rhyme scheme]
answers_sanitized: alliteration [or word forms such as alliterative; accept Alliterative Revival; accept Alliterative Morte Arthure]
rhyme [or word forms such as rhyming; accept "The Rhyming Poem" or "The Riming Poem"; accept end rhyme or other forms of rhyme; accept rhyme scheme]

bonuses.leadin: One of the four surviving manuscripts of poetry in this language ended up, for unknown reasons, in the Italian town of Vercelli ("ver-CHELL-ee"). For 10 points each:
parts: Name this language whose other poetic manuscripts include the Exeter Book. Beowulf was written in this language.

Perhaps the best-known Old English poetic manuscript is this codex, which contains the only copy of Beowulf as well as a translation of Judith. The edges of its pages were burned in the 1731 Ashburnham House fire, from which most of it was narrowly saved.
The oldest Old English poem, Caedmon's ("KIDE-mon's") Hymn, survives only in copies of this medieval scholar's work Ecclesiastical History of the English People.

Nowell Codex [or Cotton MS Vitellius A XV]
The Venerable Bede [or Saint Bede or Bæda]

parts: Name this attributed author of a five-line “Death Song.” This writer’s magnum opus relates how the cowherd Caedmon (“CAD-mun”) miraculously received the ability to sing to compose his namesake hymn, which this writer preserved.

In Bede’s (“beedʼs”) account, Caedmon (“CAD-mun”) gained the ability to sing during one of these events. One of these events “of the Rood” titles a poem, attributed to Caedmon, in which the cross narrates Jesus’s crucifixion.

Bede wrote texts of this type in the Aenigmata section of his Liber epigrammatum. “An onion” is the answer to a suggestive text of this kind in the Exeter Book, which likely inspired a contest of these things in The Hobbit.

dreams [accept The Dream of the Rood; prompt on sleeping]

bonuses.leadin: In an enigmatic poem from the Exeter Book, a woman pines for a man with this one-syllable name before addressing another man named Eadwacer (“EED-wace-er”), who may be her captor. For 10 points each:
parts: Give this syllable, which is merged with “Cyne” (“KIN-uh”) in the name of a poet whose signed poems include Elene and Juliana. This syllable ends the name of a poem preserved in the Nowell Codex about a king of the Geats (“GAY-ahts”).

The epic poem Beowulf was written in this language used by the poet Cynewulf and by the authors collected in the Exeter Book. This language developed into a later “Middle” form used to write The Canterbury Tales.

Beowulf and Elene both use a poetic trope in which a wolf, a raven, and an eagle are the namesake “beasts” of this sort of event. One of these events titles an Old English poem that depicts the “ofermōde” (“OH-fer-mode”) of the hero Byrhtnoth (“BERT-noth”).
answers: wulf [or wolf; accept Cynewulf or Beowulf; accept “Wulf and Eadwacer”]
Old English [or Anglo-Saxon; do not accept or prompt on “Middle English”]
battle [accept beasts of battle; accept “The Battle of Maldon”; do not accept or prompt on related answers like “war”]

bonuses.leadin: This collection contains a fragmentary 9th-century poem about a “strong servant of the Savior,” Judith. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this Old English manuscript, which depicts fantastic peoples such as blemmyae and hostes in the illustrated Wonders of the East. Grímur Jónsson Thorkelin transcribed Beowulf from this text.

This Old English elegy from the Exeter Book begins with the compound uhtcearu (“OOT-cha-roo”), meaning “pre-dawn-sorrow.” Its speaker predicts, “That man of mine / will drag his days under a mighty mind-caring.”

This Beowulf character is described by the similar compound term uhtfloga (“OOT-flog-uh”), meaning “dawn-flyer.” At the end of the poem, an aged Beowulf slays this character with assistance from Wiglaf.

The Wanderer, like Beowulf, includes a depiction of the “beasts of battle,” a poetic trope in which these three animals accompany warriors to feast on the bodies of the slain. Name any two of those animals.

The “elegies” of the Exeter Book, such as “The Wanderer,” explore this Anglo-Saxon concept, which is variously translated as “fate” or “personal destiny,” and the corresponding impermanence of earthly things. The first line of “The Ruin” claims that this thing broke the walls.
answers: wyrd (“wird”) [or wyrde] (The line is “Wrætlic is þes wealstan, wyrde gebræcon.”)

bonuses.leadin: This language was used to write a poem which, translated, begins: ‘This masonry is wondrous; fates broke it / courtyard pavements were smashed; the work of giants is decaying.’ For 10 points each:
parts: The title character of another poem in this language reminisces that ‘Blithe heart cannot know, / through its happiness, what hardships they suffer / who drive the foam-furrow furthest from land.’
This collection of Anglo-Saxon verse, the largest still in existence, contains The Wanderer, The Ruin, and The Seafarer, as well as 95 shorter riddles.

This fictional world takes its name from a line in the poem Christ I, which is included in the Exeter Book. The line in question is preceded by one that declares ‘Hail Earendel brightest of Angels’. This was also the name JRR Tolkien used for the central continent where most of the action of the Lord of Rings takes place.
answers: Middle Earth (Hail Earandel brightest of angels/ over Middle Earth sent to men – Eálá Earendel engla beorhtast / Ofer middangeard monnum sended.)

parts_sanitized: Name this codex, the source for such anonymous works as "The Whale", "The Wanderer", and "The Wife's Lament".
The poems in the Exeter Book were written in this language, which was also used to write the poems in the Vercelli Book and the Nowell Codex, the latter of which includes Beowulf.

The fourth major codex of Old English literature is named for this poet, though he almost certainly wrote none of the poems in it. A poem named for him a nine-line hymn describing an illiterate cow-herd singing a song of the creation of the world, and it survives in Bede's Ecclesiastical History.

bonuses.leadin: For 10 points each, answer the following about Old English poetry.
parts: These exocentric compound words are poetic devices that figuratively describe an underlying noun. In Beowulf, examples include “whale-road” and "ring-giver."
This technique was used rather than rhyme in Old English poems such as Beowulf, which employs it to link the two half-lines of a given poetic line.

This poem, found in the Exeter Book, is mostly narrated by a figure who laments his life on the “paths of exile.” It notes that "fate is inexorable" in its opening lines, a brief prologue in the author's voice.
